description: Celastrus orbiculatus is a woody vine of the family Celastraceae. It is commonly called Oriental bittersweet, as well as Chinese bittersweet, Asian bittersweet, round-leaved bittersweet, and Asiatic bittersweet.
origin: Russian Far East, China, Korea, Central   S. Japan
synonyms: ["Celastrus articulatus var. orbiculatus", "Celastrus articulatus var. humilis", "Celastrus articulatus var. papillosus", "Celastrus articulatus", "Celastrus orbiculatus f. papillosus", "Celastrus orbiculatus var. papillosus", "Celastrus insularis", "Ilex leucantha", "Celastrus orbiculatus f. aureoarillatus", "Celastrus tatarinowii"]

care.fertilizer_type: Dilute fertilizers following instructions, apply 1-2 times monthly
care.watering_guide: Missouri BG: Easily grown in most soils. Best in lean to average soils with regular moisture in full sun. Lean soils help restrain growth. Uses

Chinese bittersweet should not be planted in the eastern or midwestern U
Where appropriate, it can be an interesting addition to woodland gardens and naturalized areas
Provides quick cover for fences, arbors, trellises, posts, walls or other structures in the landscape
common_pests: Euonymus scale and two-marked treehoppers may cause significant damage in some areas

description: Celastrus scandens, commonly called American bittersweet, is a species of bittersweet that blooms mostly in June and is commonly found on rich, well-drained soils of woodlands.
origin: Central   E. Canada to N.   E.   Central U.S.A
synonyms: ["Evonymoides scandens", "Euonymus scandens", "Celastrus bullatus"]

description: Denhamia cunninghamii is a species of shrub endemic to Australia. The natural range extends from The Kimberley and throughout The Northern Territory and Queensland. The plant grows to 6 metres in height, though it commonly flowers as a much smaller shrub. It is most commonly found in savanna and open forests, though it occasionally extends into monsoon forest.
origin: NSW CT, QLD, QLD QU, WAU WA, NSW NS
synonyms: ["Celastrus cunninghamii var. parvifolius", "Maytenus cunninghamii", "Celastrus baileyanus", "Catha cunninghamii", "Celastrus cunninghamii"]

care.tips: Native to: AUSTRALIA, WAU WA, NTA OO, NTA, WAU

description: Denhamia silvestris is a shrub or small tree growing from Picton, New South Wales to Kroombit Tops, near Gladstone, Queensland. It occurs in dry rainforest, eucalyptus and rainforest ecotone areas. Common names include narrow leaved orangebark, orange bush and orange bark.

Elaeodendron australe, commonly known as red olive-berry, red-fruited olive plum, or blush boxwood, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae and is endemic to eastern Australia. It is a shrub or small tree with egg-shaped to oblong leaves with a wavy margin, yellowish green male and female flowers on separate plants and fleshy orange-red fruit.

origin: BLZ, BAH, MXE AG, LEE MO, PUE
synonyms: ["Sageretia corymbosa", "Cassine rotundata", "Ilex ternatiflora", "Cassine cubensis", "Elaeodendron xylocarpum f. amblyocarpum", "Cassine dioica", "Elaeodendron lippoldii", "Elaeodendron trichotomum", "Elaeodendron xylocarpum var. acuminatum", "Elaeodendron trinitense"]

description: Euonymus alatus, known variously as burning bush, winged euonymus, winged spindle, and winged spindle-tree, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, native to central and northern China, Japan, and Korea.
origin: South Siberia to East Asia, Japan and China.
synonyms: ["Euonymus arakianus", "Euonymus alatus var. verus", "Euonymus striatus var. pilosus", "Euonymus alatus subvar. pilosus", "Euonymus alatus subvar. subtriflorus", "Euonymus alatus subsp. sacrosanctus", "Euonymus alatus f. striatus", "Euonymus striatus var. cornucarpus", "Euonymus subtriflorus", "Euonymus alatus f. pilosus"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weakness. Heart rhythm abnormalities with large doses.
care.toxicity_symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weakness. Heart rhythm abnormalities with large doses.
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.
care.edible_parts: Use caution. Many species of this genus are toxic to humans.

description: Euonymus americanus is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae. Common names include strawberry bush, American strawberry bush, bursting-heart, hearts-a-bustin, and hearts-bustin'-with-love. It is native to the eastern United States, its distribution extending as far west as Texas. It has also been recorded in Ontario.
origin: Central   Eastern U.S.A
synonyms: ["Euonymus americanus var. angustifolius", "Euonymus scandens", "Euonymus angustifolius", "Euonymus americanus var. sarmentosus", "Euonymus muricatus", "Euonymus alternifolius", "Euonymus sarmentosus", "Euonymus sempervirens", "Euonymus heterophylus"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Deep shade (Less than 2 hours to no direct sunlight) | Climate: temperate
common_problems: No serious problems, aside from browsing by deer.
common_pests: Scale insects can be a problem.

description: The flowers are replaced by 4-lobed seed capsules that become mature during the fall. Each seed capsule splits open into four parts to expose fleshy red fruits) that each contain two seeds. The seed capsules are light pink or pale purple with a smooth surface; they later become more dark-colored. The seeds are light brown with a smooth surface.
origin: Eastern North America, Canada and Eastern United States.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.used_for: ["Bark", "leaves and fruits were formerly used for medicinal purposes; however", "all parts of the plant are poisonous if ingested."]
care.used_for_details: Bark, leaves and fruits were formerly used for medicinal purposes; however, all parts of the plant are poisonous if ingested.

description: Euonymus europaeus, the spindle, European spindle, or common spindle, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, native to much of Europe, where it inhabits the edges of forest, hedges and gentle slopes, tending to thrive on nutrient-rich, chalky and salt-poor soils. It is a deciduous shrub or small tree.
origin: Europe to Caucasus and western Asia.
synonyms: ["Euonymus angustifolius", "Euonymus europaeus var. australis", "Euonymus suberosus", "Euonymus vulgaris var. microphyllus", "Euonymus multiflorus", "Euonymus latifolius", "Euonymus quadrigonus", "Euonymus flavescens", "Euonymus fibrillifer", "Euonymus europaeus var. suberosus"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.tips: Similar plants: Ampelopsis glandulosa var. brevipedunculata
care.toxicity_note: Human: strong toxic. Animal: strong toxic. Parts: Whole plant, highest concentrations in fruit. (Source: TPPT/Agroscope)
care.toxic_parts: Whole plant, highest concentrations in fruit
care.used_for: ["The whole plant yields a volatile oil that is used in soap making. A high quality charcoal is obtained from the wood", "it is used by artists."]
care.used_for_details: The whole plant yields a volatile oil that is used in soap making. A high quality charcoal is obtained from the wood, it is used by artists.
care.edible_parts: Toxic when eaten in quantity by humans.

description: Fertile flowers are replaced by seed capsules that become mature during the autumn. Mature seed capsules are white, globoid, and about ¼ inch across. Eventually, they split open to reveal fleshy arils that are orange to red; solitary seeds occurs within these arils.
origin: East Asia, China, Japan, and Korea.
synonyms: ["Cassine fortunei", "Elaeodendron fortunei"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day) | Climate: temperate
care.edible_parts: Toxic when ingested in large quantities.

description: Euonymus hamiltonianus, known by the common names Hamilton's spindletree, Himalayan spindle, and Siebold's spindle is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae. It is native to Asia, where it is distributed in Afghanistan, Russia, China, Japan, Korea, India, Nepal, Pakistan, Bhutan, Thailand, and Myanmar. This is one of the most common Euonymus species. It is cultivated in gardens and landscapes in other parts of the world.
origin: MYA, AFG OO, CHN SA, CHS HN, JAP HN
synonyms: ["Euonymus yedoensis f. calocarpus", "Euonymus hamiltonianus var. yedoensis", "Euonymus hamiltonianus var. semiexsertus", "Euonymus juzepczukii", "Euonymus nikoensis", "Euonymus hians", "Euonymus semiexsertus", "Euonymus sieboldianus f. stenophyllus", "Euonymus sieboldianus f. dorsicostatus", "Euonymus atropurpureus"]

description: Euonymus japonicus is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, native to Japan and Korea.
origin: Korea, Japan to Nansei-shoto
synonyms: ["Euonymus japonicus var. pyramidatus", "Euonymus japonicus f. pyramidatus", "Euonymus japonicus var. argenteovariegatus", "Euonymus japonicus var. litoralis", "Euonymus japonicus f. aureomarginatus", "Euonymus japonicus f. argenteovariegatus", "Euonymus japonicus var. viridivariegatus", "Euonymus japonicus var. aureomarginatus", "Euonymus japonicus var. subinteger", "Euonymus japonicus aureus-variegatus"]

common_problems: Powdery mildew can be a problem, as can mites, leaf miners, aphids, mealybugs, and crown gall.
Additional diseases which may appear include witches  broom, stem dieback, and fungal leaf spots.
It can also become weedy in disturbed areas around houses and buildings.
common_pests: It is susceptible to euonymus scale and is frequently damaged by deer.
Powdery mildew can be a problem, as can mites, leaf miners, aphids, mealybugs, and crown gall.
Plants can be susceptible to attacks by caterpillars and are often damaged during the flowering season.
care.edible_parts: Toxic if eaten in large quantities.

description: Euonymus latifolius, the broad-leaved spindle tree, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae. It is native to the Mediterranean region, south-central Europe, the Caucasus, and the Middle East as far as Iran, and it has been introduced to Ireland, Great Britain, and Belgium. A shrub with a rounded growth form reaching 2.5 to 4 m, it is typically found in shrublands, and in rocky habitats such as inland cliffs and mountain peaks.
origin: IRE NI, YUG, IRN OO, YUG MA, ALG
synonyms: ["Kalonymus latifolia", "Euonymus europaeus var. latifolius", "Euonymus europaeus subsp. latifolius"]

care.toxicity_note: Human: strong toxic. Animal: strong toxic. Parts: Whole plant. (Source: TPPT/Agroscope)

origin: South East Siberia to China, Korea, and Japan.
synonyms: ["Euonymus hamiltonianus var. maackii", "Euonymus semipersistens", "Euonymus trapococcus", "Euonymus maackii var. trichophyllus", "Euonymus rugosus", "Euonymus quelpaertensis", "Euonymus hamiltonianus var. semipersistens", "Euonymus forbesii", "Euonymus bungeanus var. ovatus", "Euonymus bungeanus var. latifolius"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
common_pests: Euonymus scale is a problem with most species of this plant.
care.edible_parts: Although toxicity has not been recorded for this particular species, a number of plants in this genus are poisonous and so caution is advised. Illness results only after eating large quantities of any part of the shrub.

description: Euonymus macropterus is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae. It is native to southern Russian Far East, including Sakhalin and the Kuriles, Manchuria, the Korean peninsula, and Japan. A deciduous shrub reaching 3 m (10 ft) tall and equally wide, it is typically found in mixed evergreen/deciduous forests, and in scrublands, at elevations from 300 to 2,100 m.
origin: KHA OO, PRM OO, CHM HJ, JAP, SAK
synonyms: ["Kalonymus macroptera", "Euonymus ussuriensis", "Turibana macroptera"]

description: Euonymus myrianthus, the many-flowered spindle, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae. It is native to central and southern China. A variable evergreen shrub or small tree, and reaching 3 to 12 m in height, it is typically found in wooded areas and forests, from near sea level up to 1,200 m (3,900 ft).
origin: CHC CQ, CHN TJ, CHS MA, CHN GS, CHS HK
synonyms: ["Euonymus myrianthus var. tenuis", "Euonymus sargentianus", "Euonymus lipoensis", "Euonymus rosthornii", "Euonymus myrianthus var. crassifolius", "Euonymus rosthornii var. crassifolius", "Euonymus myrianthus var. tenuifolius", "Euonymus rosthornii var. tenuifolius"]

description: Euonymus obovatus, the running strawberry bush, is a trailing, woodland groundcover plant of the family Celastraceae, which is native to North America in the eastern United States and south-eastern Canada.

Euonymus oxyphyllus, the Korean spindle tree, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, native to central and eastern China, Manchuria, Korea, Japan and the Kurils. It is a shrub or small tree typically reaching 2.5 m (8 ft). The Royal Horticultural Society considers it to be a good tree for smaller gardens, especially for its colorful Autumn foliage and fruits.
origin: CHN SA, CHS HN, JAP HN, CHS AH, CHS JX
synonyms: ["Turibana yesoensis", "Pragmotessara latifolia", "Euonymus oxyphyllus var. magnus", "Euonymus oxyphyllus f. magnus", "Euonymus oxyphyllus var. nipponicus", "Euonymus oxyphyllus var. yesoensis", "Euonymus latifolius", "Euonymus nipponicus", "Euonymus laxiflorus", "Kalonymus oxyphylla"]

description: Euonymus sachalinensis, the flat-stalked spindle, is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, native to Japan, China, Korea, and the Island of Sakhalin. Growing to 2.5 m (8.2 ft) tall and broad, it is a deciduous shrub notable for its leaves turning red in autumn, and its red fruit which splits open to reveal orange seeds. Exceptional specimens, such as the one in the Hørsholm Arboretum, Copenhagen University, can become trees up to 4 m (13 ft) in height.
origin: PRM OO, GER, CHM HJ, JAP, SAK
synonyms: ["Euonymus planipes subsp. maximowiczianus", "Euonymus robustus", "Turibana sachalinensis", "Turibana tricarpa", "Euonymus oxyphyllus var. kuenburgii", "Euonymus maximowiczianus", "Turibana planipes", "Euonymus latifolius var. planipes", "Kalonymus miniata", "Euonymus latifolius var. sachalinensis"]

description: Gymnosporia buxifolia is a species of plant in the bittersweet family (Celastraceae) native to southern Africa. It is commonly known as the pioneer spike-thorn or common spike-thorn. It ranges from Angola and Mozambique to South Africa.

description: Maytenus boaria, commonly known as Maitén, is an evergreen tree in the family Celastraceae. It is native to southern South America. In Chile, it occurs approximately from 30 to 50°S. It can reach dimensions up to 20 m (66 ft) high and 80 cm (31 in) diameter, with a straight trunk.

description: Parnassia fimbriata is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae known by the common name fringed grass of Parnassus. It was first described by Charles Konig. It is native to western North America from Alaska and northwestern Canada to the southern Rocky Mountains, where it is a plant of alpine and subalpine environments, usually in wet areas. Despite the common name, this is not a true grass.
origin: Eastern USA

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Parnassia glauca, also known as fen grass of Parnassus, is a flowering herb of the genus Parnassia.
origin: Eastern USA

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

origin: Eastern USA

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Parnassia palustris, the marsh grass of Parnassus, northern grass-of-Parnassus, or just grass-of-Parnassus, and bog star, is a flowering plant in the staff-vine family Celastraceae.
origin: Eastern USA

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Paxistima myrsinites is a species of shrub in the family Celastraceae. It is native to western North America from British Columbia south to northern Mexico and east to the Rocky Mountains, where it grows in forests, often in the understory.

Prionostemma aspera is species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, and the sole species in genus Prionstemma. It is a scrambling shrub or tree native to the tropical Americas, native from Nayarit in western Mexico to Honduras, and from Costa Rica through northern South America to Bolivia and Northeastern Brazil. It grows in lowland tropical moist forests.
Pterocelastrus tricuspidatus, commonly called candlewood, cherrywood or kershout, is a medium-sized evergreen tree, indigenous to South Africa.
